query: CanSat competition rules descent rate 11 m/s diameter 30 cm parachute

results.title: ESA - What is a CanSat?
results.description: For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons.
results.content:
For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons.
It is recommended to use bright coloured fabric, which will facilitate recovery of the CanSat after landing. The parachute connection must be able to withstand up to 1000 N of force. The strength of the parachute must be tested to ensure that the system will operate nominally. For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons.
CanSat competitions have taken place in many countries around the world since the late 1990s. Typically, a number of student teams come together to launch their CanSats during the course of a day, competing for prizes. The rules and objectives can vary, but some common requirements are outlined below. ... All the components of the CanSat must fit inside a standard soda can (115 mm height and 66 mm diameter), with the exception of the parachute.
N.B. The rocket payload area has 4.5 cm of space per CanSat available, along the can’s axial dimension (i.e. height), which must accommodate all external elements including: parachute, parachute attachment hardware, and any antennas.
The challenge for the students is to fit all the major subsystems found in a satellite, such as power, sensors and a communication system, into this minimal volume. The CanSat is then launched to an altitude of a few hundred metres by a rocket or dropped from a platform or captive balloon and its mission begins: to carry out a scientific experiment and achieve a safe landing.

results.title: CanSat competition requirements - The CanSat book
results.description: For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. The descent rate is recommended to be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If not other agreements or requirements have been made).
results.content:
For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. The descent rate is recommended to be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If not other agreements or requirements have been made).
The rocket payload area usually has 4.5 cm of space per CanSat available, along the can’s axial dimension (i.e. height), which must accommodate all external elements including: parachute, parachute attachment hardware, and any antennas. For the primary mission we need to measure temperature and pressure as a function of time during the descent of the CanSat.
The antennas, transducers and other elements of the CanSat cannot extend beyond the can’s diameter until it has left the launch vehicle. For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. The descent rate is recommended to be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If not other agreements or requirements have been made).
All the components of the CanSat, with the exception of parachute, antenna and GPS, must fit inside a European soda can: 115 mm high and 66 mm diameter.
The CanSat should have a recovery system, such as a parachute, capable of being reused after launch.

results.title: Nordic CanSat Competition Guidelines | Nordic ESERO | Using Space in Education
results.description: The deployable subsystems and recovery system can exceed the length of the primary structure, but can’t be deployed before the CanSat is fully ejected from the rocket. Flight time is limited to 120 sec (If not other agreements have been made). The descent rate must be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If ...
results.content:
The deployable subsystems and recovery system can exceed the length of the primary structure, but can’t be deployed before the CanSat is fully ejected from the rocket. Flight time is limited to 120 sec (If not other agreements have been made). The descent rate must be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If ...
The CanSat should have a recovery system, such as a parachute or other mechanism for reducing decent rate. The antennas, transducers and other elements of the CanSat cannot extend beyond the can’s diameter until it has left the rocket. The deployable subsystems and recovery system can exceed the length of the primary structure, but can’t be deployed before the CanSat is fully ejected from the rocket. Flight time is limited to 120 sec (If not other agreements have been made). The descent rate must be between 8 m/s and 11m/s (If not other agreements have been made).
In addition to the space theme, there is a list of technical requirements for the CanSat which has to be passed before flight (for example weight and size) and also some requirements during flight (for example decent speed). All the components (with the exception of parachute, antenna and GPS) of the CanSat must fit inside a European soda can: 115 mm height and 66 mm diameter.
During the CanSat operation, each team will get their CanSats launched up to an altitude of approximately 1 000 meter by a rocket. At this altitude the CanSats will be ejected from the rocket. During the descent, the CanSats will perform their missions and send data to the ground trough a radio transmitter.
The CanSat must be able to withstand an acceleration of up to 20G.

It is recommended to use bright coloured fabric, which will facilitate recovery of the CanSat after landing. The parachute connection must be able to withstand up to 50 N of force. The strength of the parachute must be tested to ensure that the system will operate nominally. For recovery reasons, a maximum flight time of 120 seconds is recommended. If attempting a directed landing, then a maximum of 170 seconds flight time is recommended. A descent rate between 8 and 11 m/s is recommended for recovery reasons.
All the components of the CanSat must fit inside a standard soft drinks can (115 mm height and 66 mm diameter), with the exception of the parachute. Radio antennas and GPS antennas can be mounted externally on the top or bottom of the can, depending on the design, but not on the sides.
Note: The rocket payload area usually has 4.5 cm of space per CanSat available, along the can’s axial dimension (i.e. height), which must accommodate all external elements including: parachute, parachute attachment hardware, and any antennas.

results.title: Parachute design - The CanSat book
results.description: When the velocity grows, the drag force becomes larger and after a few seconds the drag force from the parachute will reach equilibrium with the force of gravity. From that point on, the acceleration will be zero and the CanSat will descend at a constant velocity. This constant velocity has to be larger than the minimum descent velocity specified in the requirements.
results.content:
When the velocity grows, the drag force becomes larger and after a few seconds the drag force from the parachute will reach equilibrium with the force of gravity. From that point on, the acceleration will be zero and the CanSat will descend at a constant velocity. This constant velocity has to be larger than the minimum descent velocity specified in the requirements.
Both will enhance the stability of the CanSat. Before we can start making the parachute we will have to figure out how big it should be. More specifically, we need to calculate how much surface area the parachute will need in order to fulfil the requirements. Logic suggests that the bigger the parachute the slower the object’s descent velocity.
Although it would be very beneficial for the CanSat to have a very low descent rate, a limit has been set to ensure that the CanSat will land near the launch area. If the descent rate is too slow the CanSat may drift kilometres away along with the wind, which is neither allowed nor desired.
During the descent two forces will be acting on the CanSat as illustrated in the figure to the right. Gravity will pull on the can and accelerate it towards the ground, and the drag force on the parachute will act on the CanSat in the opposite direction and slows down the descent rate.

results.title: 1 CanSat 2022 Preliminary Design Review (PDR) Team 1078 PWr Aerospace
results.description: CanSat deployment from rocket compartment at 670-725 metres. Descent using parachute; between peak altitude and 400 meters maintaining descent rate at 10-20 m/s.
results.content:
CanSat deployment from rocket compartment at 670-725 metres. Descent using parachute; between peak altitude and 400 meters maintaining descent rate at 10-20 m/s.
Video from container shall record payload descent. ... Hemispherical parachutes. ... Deployment of second parachute. ... Flat hexagonal parachutes. At ... Container will be fully 3D printed. ... Conclusion:The most important goal when designing CanSat was to ensure stability.
At altitude of 400 meters deployment of the larger parachute; descent rate slowed down to 3-7 m/s. Payload deployment at 300 meters; unwinding 10 meter tether in 20 seconds from container. Container must send telemetry to ground station (1 Hz); after payload release it should transfer telemetry polled from ... Recording from payload camera pointing at south (+/- 20 degrees) and 45 degrees up from payload NADIR direction. Camera must have resolution of at least 640x480 pixels and record descent with more than 30 frames per second in color.
The descent will be recorded ... 8. Upon payload release, container shall start polling data from payload. After first telemetry send, payload camera will start ... Container wall will be fully 3D printed and wrapped in a foil. Existing · (on top and bottom of the container) edges will be rounded up to · minimize risk of failure during deployment. There is no additional · protrusion. Parachutes are hidden in the parachute bay.
The rocket airframe shall not be used as part of the CanSat operations. ... All structures shall be built to survive 15 Gs of launch acceleration. ... All structures shall be built to survive 30 Gs of shock.
